serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled bamboo shoots is approximately 120g (about 1 cup), commonly served as a side vegetable or incorporated into stir-fries and soups.

nutrient_source.title: Bamboo shoots, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040022, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for boiled bamboo shoots (FDC ID 169226) for secondary nutrient fields. Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Vegetables — analogous low-GI non-starchy vegetable estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for boiled bamboo shoots exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Bamboo shoots are a non-starchy vegetable with very low carbohydrate content (1.92g/100g). GI estimated at approximately 20 (low) by analogy with other low-carbohydrate non-starchy vegetables (e.g., asparagus, green beans) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 120g serving = (20 × 1.92 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 0.5, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (533mg/100g) providing a highly favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Boiled bamboo shoots are strongly suitable for hypertension management; their negligible sodium content and very high potassium (533mg/100g) support vasodilation and blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH diet principles, provided they are not prepared with high-sodium sauces or sourced from sodium-added canned products.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.92g/100g), low glycemic index (~20), and dietary fibre (1.0g/100g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled bamboo shoots are well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low carbohydrate content, estimated low GI of approximately 20, and dietary fibre that slows glucose absorption; they should be consumed without sugary sauces or starchy thickeners to preserve their glycaemic benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.05g/100g), negligible cholesterol (0mg/100g), high potassium (533mg/100g), and low caloric density (12 kcal/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled bamboo shoots are cardioprotective due to their negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, high potassium supporting vascular health, and very low caloric density; they are a suitable vegetable for heart-healthy dietary patterns and may be consumed freely as part of a cardiovascular disease prevention diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of strong anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, >3g fibre) despite low pro-inflammatory profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled bamboo shoots are low in pro-inflammatory components and provide potassium and dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome immune regulation, but lack sufficient anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or >3g/100g fibre) to be classified as actively beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; they are neutral and may be included in an anti-inflammatory diet when paired with omega-3-rich foods or anti-inflammatory spices.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled bamboo shoots are the tender inner shoots of bamboo plants (genus Phyllostachys or Bambusa), harvested before the sheath is removed and cooked by boiling in water until soft. The result is a pale yellow, mildly sweet, and slightly crunchy vegetable with a delicate earthy flavour and soft fibrous texture. They are widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore, China, Japan, and Thailand, and are commonly used in stir-fries, soups, braised dishes, and salads. Nutritionally, boiled bamboo shoots are exceptionally low in calories (12 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (1.92g/100g), making them one of the lowest-calorie vegetables available. They are notable for their very high potassium content (533mg/100g), which supports blood pressure regulation, and their negligible sodium (4mg/100g), making them ideal for DASH-diet and hypertension management. Their low glycemic index (~20) and minimal sugar content make them suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dietary fibre (1.0g/100g) supports digestive health and gut microbiome diversity. Boiled bamboo shoots are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and are suitable for low-calorie, low-sodium, and ketogenic dietary patterns. Canned bamboo shoots should be rinsed before use to reduce added sodium.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040022, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Secondary nutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169226) for boiled bamboo shoots, with consistent results. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to non-starchy vegetables, clearly documented.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; bamboo shoots are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 and Vitamin D are 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. Trans fat is 0.0 — naturally zero for a minimally processed plant vegetable. Vitamin A is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for boiled bamboo shoots. Saturated fat (0.05g), monounsaturated fat (0.02g), polyunsaturated fat (0.09g), sugar (0.91g), vitamin C (1.5mg), B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water (95.5g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169226 (bamboo shoots, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) as HPB Foodo does not report these secondary fields. GI value of approximately 20 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database methodology for non-starchy low-carbohydrate vegetables; no direct GI measurement for boiled bamboo shoots was found in the database as of 2026-04-16. Canned bamboo shoots typically contain added sodium and should be rinsed before use. The input record listed rheumatoid_arthritis suitability as false with a contradictory reason; this has been corrected to a moderate concern level with neutral classification in health_context.
